transcript: I'm Brennan from Anza. We're going to have a few Anza folks here; I'm just going to kick stuff off and cover things at a high level. So, as you all know, at Anza we're heavily involved in steering core protocol development. Most notably, we build the Agave client. And during the next like nine and a half minutes, we're going to go on a journey and recap a few exciting wins, but more importantly illuminate the path of where we're taking the protocol. And we'll structure things by talking about these categories on the screen, one at a time. And we'll start with resiliency. So that's almost two years of solid green liveness, around 200 billion transactions, and, you know, the message is Solana stays up, right? And it keeps servicing transactions. And that's been the case for a long time now. And I think October 10th really served as a proof point of just the level of maturity of the Agave client, but also the Solana ecosystem and its ability to handle immense transaction load and keep packing thousands of user transactions per second. And, you know, through all of this, we've kept the Agave quarterly releases rolling out like clockwork. The 3.1 release is coming up next before 4.0 brings a bunch of new exciting fun stuff in the new year. And the reason we're able to keep this aggressive cadence is because of the immense stress testing and red teaming that we perform. The Invalidator team at Anza is attacking the public testnet with a unique set of attacks triggering every hour. And we haven't been super open with what this team does for, you know, what I assume are obvious reasons, but we've fixed enough of the problems at this point that, you know, I think we can give this team a little bit of shine. So, the attacks mostly fall into two categories. We've got network DoS attacks, and we're stressing load shedding, back pressure handling, things of that nature. And the second bucket is these highly curated blocks packing with super strange transactions, stressing the far corners of the SVM and replay execution. And this graph just shows our malicious testnet nodes cycling through those attacks. Here's an example of a malicious node hosing down a bunch of the other nodes with gigabits per second of network traffic, but the network was still screaming along, making blocks on time, tower height steady at 32, all the things we want. But this wasn't always the case, right? We used to be able to make nodes crash, then we got a little better, then we could disrupt nodes, then we got a little more better, and now, you know, we can handle these things without a hitch. And it's only this level of paranoia and obsession that helps us provide the confidence that it takes to ship this aggressively. And we've been shipping a lot. 2025 has been the year of IBRL on Solana, and we've got a lot to talk about here. So let's start by talking about the major resource bottlenecks. We have about 4x headroom on compute side, so plenty of excess there. Similar story on disk for the typical case, we've got a lot of excess capacity there. Memory, this has really been the story of this year; we've cleaned up so many things when it comes to memory, so we've created headroom there as well. And on the network side, we've got a lot of validators running 10 gig or 25 gig NICs, and so we've got lots of headroom there as well, assuming we load the dang thing properly. And we were able to translate this into 25% throughput improvements on Solana mainnet this year. And even if we're not always filling the blocks, having that burst capacity is super important, right? You get an economic activity like a, you know, as simple as a market open, the blocks are full, it's really nice not to leak that economic activity. And as I'm sure most of you know, throughput is governed by block limits. So we raised the overall limit from 48 million to 60 million CUs this year. And we tend to meter on these compute unit things. So every BPF instruction, serializing data, precompiles, you know, all this stuff gets translated into CUs. We also linearize account data loading into CUs for reasons. I mean, I could rant on this stuff for a while, but I think the bottom line is we want to constrain things so that we can keep slots coming out every 400 milliseconds. So why haven't we activated the 100 million CU increase yet? Well, the key feature enabling this is creating a kernel bypass for the network layer. And it's funny because we often talk about network being the bottleneck for blockchains or like the Solana blockchain, but it's kind of not true actually at the moment when it comes to Solana. I wish it was true, but, you know, we're actually not saturating the NICs most of the time. But we do often max out on slamming the sendmmsg syscall, allocating memory in the kernel, and context switching. And XDP bypasses this by allowing us to interact with the NIC from userspace and increase throughput of this performance bottleneck by 200x. And this chart shows that latency difference between the legacy Agave code in blue and the new and improved zero-copy XDP code, the purple line down at the bottom. And notice that we had to make the Y-axis log scale so it would even show up. I mean, this thing is crazy fast. And call your validator today, this is in 3.0, tell them to turn it on, right? We need critical adoption and we'll get those 100 million CUs. There's another important block space limit that we've increased, and that's we've doubled the amount of throughput to the hottest accounts. And these are often the most popular assets, or it might just be the pump.fun global volume accumulator, I don't know, but I digress. And this chart shows when we doubled that limit from 12 to 24 million and how users immediately gobbled it up. In addition to increasing the block capacity, we've also increased the ingestion TPS, oftentimes sustaining 100k TPS on mainnet. And this is essentially a measure of, you know, how efficiently we can move bidders into the auction house, which is really essential for building efficient markets because to have an efficient market, the highest bid needs to win. For it to win, you have to actually look at all the bids, right? And this used to give us a lot of problems because allocating all the memory for all these packets caused a lot of churn and a lot of issues, but we've been able to overcome a lot of that. But this is the historical reason for why we used to use stake connections, stake-weighted QoS for managing this, so we had something else as kind of a heuristic for quality transaction flow. And speaking of connections and ingest, we're making some changes to the default limits. So we are increasing the overall ingestion constants to a million packets per second. We're increasing the number of unstaked connections from 500 to 4,000. And we're unthrottling the per-connection packet limits when global congestion is low. So lots of good fun things coming to get more transactions in. And finally, Alpenglow is coming. And Alpenglow, of course, is the new consensus engine that we're dropping in to replace Tower. It reduces transaction finality times down to 150 milliseconds, comes with increased safety, and enables us to have some much more intelligent ways of defeating the slot laggers. We first announced Alpenglow in the middle of this year, but we've made a bunch of progress. So we've been running a 50-node globally distributed test cluster under load for the last more than four months. And we've had so few problems, I like fail to believe it sometimes, I had to get in there make sure it's actually running stuff, but the team's really been cooking on this one doing amazing stuff. And we're working to get this feature complete and rolled out on testnet early next year. And this also sets the foundation for a bunch more interesting stuff, such as better market structure. So Solana showed everyone that blockchains can be fast and affordable, and now we're going to show them that they can be fair and highly efficient while remaining trustless. So how are we going to do it? It's Multiple Concurrent Proposers, or MCP. We're ending the single builder monopoly. Users and dApps want optionality and regional network latencies. Traders want to have censorship resistance. And MCP is going to deliver all these things. And there's no middleman here; this is all the validator just playing different roles, right? So, yeah, just keeping that pipeline flowing from the client. And, you know, we're already incrementally building towards this vision. We'll start shipping features to move closer to this ideal state with each release in the coming future. And by the way, I've been collecting the receipts of everyone doubting us saying that MCP is going to take two years. Spite-driven development is the most amazing thing ever, thank you for fueling us. All right, the last thing we'll talk about is DevEx and OpEx. So we've heard the cries from the community: the rent is too damn high and the transactions are too damn small. And so we're going to fix this. We are lowering the cost of state on the blockchain to make it more affordable for projects to bootstrap lots of users, lots of accounts, all that good stuff. I believe the state bond is somewhere around a million dollars per gigabyte on mainnet, which, I mean, you get a lot of replication on Solana, but still like NVMes are what, 10 cents a gig? So this is a little crazy, we're going to do better here. And we're also increasing the transaction size up to 4KB. So this is going to allow us to touch more accounts without using ALTs, and be able to craft more complex atomic operations in protocol. On the operational side, we're going to stop wearing out your disks so fast. We're making restarts faster. And we're going to do this by slowing down the incremental snapshot frequency, using wincode instead of bincode for faster serdes, and being smarter with what we actually write to disk. Yeah, shout out wincode. Yeah, thank you. All right, and finally, SIMD-123 is going to ship. And so we're going to make it simpler for validators to share revenue from different sources with stakers and infra providers in protocol. Delegated stake directly increases the number of blocks you get. Those blocks can be valuable. We're bringing a lot of trading activity to Solana, and so it's important that we have a, you know, fair way of sharing those profits with stakers. All right. That was a whirlwind look at all the exciting protocol changes that are coming to Solana. Thanks for listening. You should now be properly amped up for all this cool stuff that we're going to ship next year. At Anza, we don't do roadmaps, we do releases. Follow me on X to stay up on all things Agave. Tune into the Anza blogs. And as always, the real alpha exists in plain sight on GitHub. All right, thanks everyone.

# Base–Solana Bridge Goes Live with Chainlink CCIP, Unlocking Massive Cross-Chain Liquidity
![Liam Turner](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%2048%2048%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)
By[Liam Turner](https://www.cryptoninjas.net/authors/liam/)
December 6, 2025
* 3min read
![base-solana](https://www.cryptoninjas.net/wp-content/uploads/base-solana.jpeg)
**Key Takeaways:**
* The Base–Solana Bridge is now live on mainnet, secured jointly by Chainlink CCIP and Coinbase.
* Users and developers can transfer SOL and SPL tokens to Base apps, enabling unified liquidity across both ecosystems.
* The launch marks one of the most significant cross-chain expansions for Base, strengthening its position as a hub for multichain Web3 activity.
The long-anticipated Base–Solana Bridge has officially[launched](https://x.com/chainlink/status/1996657107597160887?s=20), creating one of the most consequentia

sources.title: Block, Anthropic, and OpenAI Launch the Agentic AI Foundation
sources.snippet:
Block - Block, Anthropic, and OpenAI Launch the Agentic AI Foundation
Block logo
December 09, 2025
# Block, Anthropic, and OpenAI Launch the Agentic AI Foundation
[](https://linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://block.xyz/inside/block-anthropic-and-openai-launch-the-agentic-ai-foundation)[](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://block.xyz/inside/block-anthropic-and-openai-launch-the-agentic-ai-foundation&via=blocks)
![Block, Anthropic, and OpenAI Launch the Agentic AI Foundation](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/8m3angk4/production/bc19584a9da572a1a0bb5e2e8ae9413256f791eb-1920x800.png?w=420&auto=format)
Author
Block
Tags
[Open Source](https://block.xyz/news/open-source),[AI](https://block.xyz/news/ai),[Technology](https://block.xyz/news/technology),[Block](https://block.xyz/news/block)
Author
Block
Tags
[Open Source](https://block.xyz/news/open-source),[AI](https://block.xyz/news/ai),[Technology](https://block.xyz/news/technology),[Block](https://block.xyz/news/block)
An initiative to advance open source agentic AI under the Linux Foundation umbrella
Today, the industry is making a pivotal choice. Block, Anthropic, OpenAI, and other leaders in AI are launching the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) to ensure agentic AI develops as an open, collaborative ecosystem. Agentic AI refers to artificial intelligence systems that can take initiative, make decisions, and act independently to achieve goals with minimal human direction.
The AAIF is a vendor-neutral home for open source agentic AI projects where no single company dominates, providing funding for critical community programs and research, and building open protocols that let systems from different builders work together seamlessly. Block is contributing its open source agent**[goose](https://block.github.io/goose/)**to the AAIF, along with Anthropic’s[**Model Context Protocol**](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/getting-started/intro)[**(MCP)**](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/getting-started/intro)and OpenAI’s[**AGENTS.md**](http://agents.md).
In addition to the three founding members, the AAIF’s platinum members include Amazon Web Services, Bloomberg, Cloudflare, Google, and Microsoft. Gold members of the AAIF include Adyen, Arcade.dev, Cisco, Datadog, Docker, Ericsson, IBM, JetBrains, Okta, Oracle, Runlayer, Salesforce, SAP, Shopify, Snowflake, Temporal, Tetrate, and Twilio Inc. Silver members of the AAIF include Apify, Chronosphere, Cosmonic, Elasticsearch, Eve Security, Hugging Face, Kubermatic, KYXStart, LanceDB, Mirantis, NinjaTech AI, Obot.ai, Prefect.io, Pydantic, Shinkai.com, Solo.io, Spectro Cloud, Stacklok, SUSE, Uber, WorkOS, Zapier and ZED.
##### Why This Matters Now
AI systems represent one of the most significant technological shifts in decades. These systems are fundamentally reshaping how developers build software, how businesses operate, and how people solve complex problems. Use of autonomous agents promises to accelerate this even further.
But we're at a critical juncture. We have an unprecedented opportunity to shape how this technology develops. Will we build open, interoperable infrastructure that serves everyone? Or will we see fragmentation that limits this powerful technology's potential? Without collaborative open development, we risk missing the full potential of agentic AI where everyone benefits thanks to open standards, open protocols, and open systems on which other tools are built. We also risk concentrating the power in the hands of a few, which dampers competition and restricts accessibility. In a proprietary, siloed ecosystem, breakthrough ideas can't easily build on each other, integration challenges slow enterprise adoption and limit use cases, accessibility barriers prevent smaller organizations from benefiting, and research limitations slow academic and independent innovation.
History proves there’s a better path. The Internet, Linux, and the Web succeeded precisely because they were open. They empowered anyone w

tags.impact_reason: The update on Agave client development and resilience improvements are solid, but not ecosystem-shattering, thus a moderate score.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a crystalline mountain range as the Semantic Anchor because the upgrade is named 'Alpenglow,' which is the reddish glow on mountain peaks. This metaphor connects the upgrade's name to its function as a foundational, resilient overhaul for the Solana network. The mountain represents stability and scale, while the crystalline material signifies the digital, precise nature of consensus. I included the text 'IBRL' (Increasing Bandwidth, Reducing Latency) as a diegetic holographic etching on the mountain face, directly referencing the core technical standard mentioned in the standfirst and fitting the high-tech, engineering vibe.
visual_idea.description: A vast mountain range at dawn, but the mountains are not made of rock. They are monolithic structures of interlocking polished obsidian and translucent crystalline blocks, suggesting a massive, geological-scale server farm. A brilliant, warm orange and pink light—the 'alpenglow'—catches the highest peaks, creating sharp highlights and deep shadows. In the foreground, subtly etched onto the face of the central peak, the acronym 'IBRL' glows with a soft, holographic light, as if it's a system-level engineering designation. The background fades into a deep, dark digital void with faint, out-of-focus network grid lines, emphasizing immense scale.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, vertical composition (3:4 aspect ratio) showcasing a vast mountain range at dawn. The mountains are not made of rock, but are monolithic structures of interlocking polished obsidian and translucent crystalline blocks, meticulously crafted to suggest a massive, geological-scale server farm. A brilliant, warm orange and pink 'alpenglow' volumetric sunrise casts cinematic golden-hour lighting, catching the highest peaks with high dynamic range, creating sharp highlights and deep, dramatic shadows. In the foreground, subtly etched onto the face of the central peak, the acronym 'IBRL' glows with a soft, holographic light, as if it's a system-level engineering designation physically manifested. The background fades into a deep, dark digital void with faint, out-of-focus network grid lines, emphasizing immense, epic scale and height, with substantial negative space at the top. 8k resolution, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, 3D Conceptual Art.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a crystalline mountain range as the Semantic Anchor because the upgrade is named 'Alpenglow,' which is the reddish glow on mountain peaks. This metaphor connects the upgrade's name to its function as a foundational, resilient overhaul for the Solana network. The mountain represents stability and scale, while the crystalline material signifies the digital, precise nature of consensus. I included the text 'IBRL' (Increasing Bandwidth, Reducing Latency) as a diegetic holographic etching on the mountain face, directly referencing the core technical standard mentioned in the standfirst and fitting the high-tech, engineering vibe.
visual_idea.description: An extreme macro shot focusing on the intersection point of several obsidian and crystalline blocks from the mountain. The polished obsidian surface perfectly reflects the warm 'alpenglow' light. Through the translucent crystal, we can see intricate, faintly glowing data pathways and nodes pulsing rhythmically deep within. The image captures the precise, complex, and layered nature of the new consensus mechanism without any text.

final_prompt.prompt_text: An extreme macro shot, 1:1 aspect ratio, focusing precisely on the symmetrical intersection point of multiple monolithic obsidian and crystalline blocks, filling the entire frame with rich texture. The highly polished obsidian surfaces perfectly reflect the warm, ethereal alpenglow light, creating cinematic golden-hour reflections. Through the translucent crystalline structures, intricate, faintly glowing data pathways and nodes are visible, pulsing rhythmically deep within, forming a complex and layered monolithic data-scape. The composition emphasizes a central focus with tight macro framing. This image captures the precise, complex, and layered nature of a new consensus mechanism, devoid of any text. ultra-photorealistic, V6 realism, high dynamic range, 8k resolution, hyper-detailed, octane render, raytracing, realistic materials, natural color grading, cinematic contrast.

editorial.headline: Anza Unveils Alpenglow Consensus and Multiple Concurrent Proposers for Solana
editorial.standfirst: Core developers commit to the "IBRL" standard—Increasing Bandwidth, Reducing Latency—with a complete consensus overhaul and kernel-level networking upgrades. The move signals a shift from raw throughput to institutional-grade reliability and market fairness.
key_takeaways: Alpenglow Consensus: A new engine replacing Tower BFT targets 150ms finality, currently running on a 50-node global test cluster.
Kernel Bypass (XDP): New networking layer delivers a 200x performance boost in packet processing, paving the way for 100 million CU blocks.
Market Structure: Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP) will end the single-leader monopoly, enhancing censorship resistance and transaction inclusion.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
Brennan Watt, VP of Core Engineering at **Anza**, delivered a technical state-of-the-union defined by a single acronym: **IBRL** (Increase Bandwidth, Reduce Latency). While the market often focuses on user-facing metrics, Anza’s 2025 roadmap reveals a fundamental re-architecture of the Solana client. The most significant revelation is **Alpenglow**, a new consensus engine designed to replace Tower BFT. Currently live on a 50-node globally distributed test cluster, Alpenglow targets transaction finality of 150 milliseconds. For institutional capital and high-frequency trading firms, this sub-second finality is the threshold required to move from experimental pilots to production-grade settlement layers.

To support this speed, Anza is aggressively removing legacy bottlenecks deep in the stack. Watt highlighted the implementation of **XDP (eXpress Data Path)**, a kernel bypass technology that allows the validator client to interact directly with the Network Interface Card (NIC) from userspace. This upgrade circumvents the operating system's kernel for packet processing, yielding a massive 200x improvement in throughput for specific bottlenecks. This optimization is the critical unlock for raising the block compute limit from 60 million to 100 million Compute Units (CUs), effectively widening the pipe for global transaction volume.

Beyond raw performance, Anza is overhauling Solana's market structure to address centralization risks. The introduction of **Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP)** aims to end the "single builder monopoly," allowing multiple leaders to propose blocks simultaneously within a slot. This architectural shift significantly improves censorship resistance and ensures fair transaction inclusion, a key concern for DeFi protocols and arbitrageurs. Watt noted that despite skepticism—referencing his "spite-driven development" in response to doubts about the timeline—the team is incrementally shipping features to support this transition.

Operational efficiency and economic alignment round out the major updates. The shift from Bincode to **Wincode** for serialization and the introduction of **SIMD-123** demonstrate a focus on sustainability. SIMD-123 enables protocol-level revenue sharing, allowing validators to automatically distribute priority fees and MEV rewards to stakers. Combined with a planned reduction in state rent costs and an increase in transaction size to 4KB, these changes lower the barrier to entry for complex applications while ensuring the network's economic engines properly incentivize participation.
